ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pasta al pesto genovese; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pasta al pesto; 'Pasta, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 229961009 'Pasta (substance)' is the closest available term; no specific pesto pasta concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of pasta al pesto genovese is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 180 g of cooked pasta and 120 g of pesto sauce with garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 20121) combined with Pesto sauce, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172949), blended at approximately 60% pasta / 40% pesto by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for pasta al pesto genovese as a composite dish. Values are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 20121) at 60% weight and USDA SR Legacy pesto sauce (FDC ID 172949) at 40% weight, reflecting a typical restaurant-style ratio. This is a secondary global source (USDA SR Legacy) used because no primary regional source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia, FAO/INFOODS SEA) covers this Italian dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pasta, cooked al dente, GI = 44 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 44 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat pasta cooked al dente (glucose reference scale). Pasta al pesto genovese uses the same pasta base; the pesto sauce contains negligible additional glycemic carbohydrates. This is the closest defensible analogous food. GL per serving calculated as: (44 × 25.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 33.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g; ~630 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a restaurant portion of pasta al pesto genovese can contribute approximately 630 mg sodium, representing roughly 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~33.5 per 300 g serving) despite low GI (44) due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although pasta al pesto genovese has a low glycemic index of 44, the glycemic load per standard restaurant serving is approximately 33.5, which is high; people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–180 g cooked pasta and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the fat and protein from pesto partially attenuate but do not eliminate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile from olive oil and pine nuts is favourable, but total fat and caloric density are elevated
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pasta al pesto genovese provides predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from extra virgin olive oil and pine nuts, which are consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, the caloric density (~218 kcal per 100 g) and saturated fat from Parmesan cheese (~2.1 g per 100 g) warrant portion moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta al Pesto Genovese is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from the Liguria region of northwestern Italy, particularly the city of Genoa. It consists of cooked wheat pasta — traditionally trofie, linguine, or spaghetti — tossed with pesto genovese, a cold-blended sauce made from fresh Genovese basil (Ocimum basilicum), pine nuts (or occasionally walnuts), garlic, extra virgin olive oil, Parmigiano-Reggiano, and Pecorino Sardo cheese. The dish is characterised by its vibrant green colour, herbaceous and nutty aroma, and a rich yet fresh flavour profile. The texture is smooth and coating, with the oil-emulsified sauce clinging to the pasta surface. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~218 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful carbohydrate load from pasta (~25 g per 100 g), and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from olive oil and pine nuts. The glycemic index of the pasta base is low (GI ~44), though the glycemic load per restaurant serving is high due to portion size. Clinically, the dish is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (moderate glycemic load per serving), hypertension (moderate sodium from cheese and any added salt), and cardiovascular disease (beneficial unsaturated fats offset by caloric density). In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and is popular as a lunch or dinner main course. It is vegetarian but not vegan, contains gluten and dairy allergens, and may contain tree nuts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pesto pasta?
Is pesto pasta good for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in pesto genovese?
Is pesto pasta high in sodium?
Can I eat pesto pasta if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of pasta al pesto?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia, FAO/INFOODS SEA) covers pasta al pesto genovese as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for cooked pasta and pesto sauce at a 60:40 weight ratio, which is a reasonable but not exact representation of the restaurant dish. GI is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white wheat pasta (analogous estimate). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy by blending cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 20121) at 60% and pesto sauce (FDC ID 172949) at 40% by weight. This ratio reflects a typical restaurant plating but has not been verified by laboratory analysis of the specific Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore dish. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for both component foods — naturally zero for unprocessed pasta and cold-blended pesto sauce. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta or pesto sauce. 4. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat pasta cooked al dente; no direct GI measurement exists for pasta al pesto genovese as a composite dish. 5. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (44 × 25.4 × 300) / 10000 = 33.5. 6. The food_group is retained as 'Grains and staples' from the input record, which is appropriate given that pasta is the primary component by weight. 7. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Italian dish and are left null; the multilingual G6 rule applies only to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes. 8. Halal status is listed as true based on the standard ingredients (pasta, basil, olive oil, pine nuts, cheese); however, restaurant-specific halal certification has not been verified for Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.
